title: Angel food cake
subtitle: American fat-free egg-white sponge with a light, springy crumb
excerpt: A very light, low-fat American sponge made from beaten egg whites with no yolks and no added fat. The batter is baked in an ungreased tube tin so the foam can cling to the sides and rise freely. The tin is inverted immediately after baking and left to cool upside down, which prevents the delicate foam structure from collapsing under its own weight. Serve with fresh berries and a little whipped cream.
sourceNotes: Angel food cake is documented in American cookery literature from the late nineteenth century. The earliest known printed recipe appears in Mrs. D.A. Lincoln, Boston Cooking School Cookbook (1884, Project Gutenberg). The recipe is elaborated in Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896, Project Gutenberg), which gives proportions consistent with the modern version. The name is attributed to the cake's white, cloud-like appearance. The ungreased-tin and inverted-cooling method are described in both sources as essential to the recipe.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake a day ahead and store at room temperature in the tin, still inverted, covered with a clean cloth. Unmould just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 155°C. Bake for 38 to 42 minutes until the surface is golden and springs back when lightly pressed. A skewer should come out clean.

recipeTools.notes: 25cm tube tin (angel food cake tin), UNGREASED

glossaryTerms.term: Inverting angel food cake to cool
glossaryTerms.definition: Turning the tin upside down immediately after baking and leaving the cake to cool completely in this position, suspended above the work surface on the tin's feet (or propped on a bottle neck through the central tube). The ungreased tin grips the cake and holds it in place while the foam structure, which is still fragile from the oven, firms up as it cools. If cooled upright, the weight of the cake would compress and partially collapse the air cells in the base, producing a dense, fallen layer. The cake is only removed from the tin once fully cool.

content.text: Two rules make or break angel food cake: do not grease the tin, and

attrs.term: Inverting angel food cake to cool

content.text: . The batter must cling to the ungreased sides to climb the tin; any grease prevents this and the cake collapses. Any trace of yolk, fat, or grease on the bowl or whisk will prevent the whites from reaching stiff peaks.

attrs.prepNote: divided: 100g for the whites, 100g sifted with the flour

content.text: Heat the oven to 175°C conventional (155°C fan). Do not grease the tin. Sift together the flour, 100g of the sugar, and the salt three times and set aside. In a spotlessly clean stand mixer bowl, whisk the egg whites and cream of tartar on medium speed until foamy. Increase to medium-high and gradually add the remaining 100g of sugar, one tablespoon at a time, whisking between each addition. Add the vanilla. Continue whisking until the whites reach firm, glossy peaks that curl at the tip but hold their shape.

Sift a quarter of the flour-sugar mixture over the whites and fold in gently with a large metal spoon using broad, sweeping strokes. Repeat with the remaining flour in three more additions; fold until just combined with no visible flour streaks. Spoon gently into the ungreased tin and smooth the top. Run a knife or skewer through the batter in a zigzag pattern to eliminate large air pockets.

Bake for 38 to 42 minutes until the surface is golden and springs back when pressed. Immediately invert the tin over a rack (the tin has raised feet for this) or hang it upside down on a bottle. Leave inverted until completely cool, at least 1 hour. Run a knife around the outer edge and the central tube, then unmould.
